vBRIEFInfo.description: Scope vBRIEF ingested from GitHub issue #1538

plan.title: Linux installer --yes reports missing core tools instead of bootstrapping them

narratives.Description: The Linux installer currently detects missing core tools during --yes installs but only reports fallback instructions, leaving a fresh WSL or Linux consumer with a doctor-failing install. This story makes the non-interactive Linux path either bootstrap the required core tools safely or fail loudly before claiming success, so first-session setup is not left in a broken state.
narratives.Overview:
## Summary
The published Linux installer succeeds in WSL, but in `--yes` mode it does not install/bootstrap the core follow-up tools it detects as missing. It reports `uv`, `task`, and `gh` as missing, with doctor also warning on `go` and `node`.

## Repro
Environment: fresh WSL2 Ubuntu, default user `msadams`.

Commands:

```bash
curl -L -o /home/msadams/install-linux-amd64 https://github.com/deftai/directive/releases/latest/download/install-linux-amd64
chmod +x /home/msadams/install-linux-amd64
rm -rf /home/msadams/repos/deft/installer-smoke
mkdir -p /home/msadams/repos/deft/installer-smoke
/home/msadams/install-linux-amd64 --yes --repo-root /home/msadams/repos/deft/installer-smoke --json
```

Observed installer version:

```text
deft-install v0.42.1
```

## Observed
The install itself exits 0 and deposits `.deft/core`, `AGENTS.md`, `.agents/skills`, `.githooks`, `Taskfile.yml`, `vbrief/`, and guard files correctly. However, output includes:

```text
Missing core tools (consent implied by --yes): gh, task, uv
  Fallbacks (run manually or via platform package manager):
    Windows: winget install --id <ID> or scripts/setup_windows.ps1
    macOS:   brew install go-task uv python gh
    Linux:   apt/brew equivalent for task uv python3 gh
```

The doctor handoff then reports:

```json
{"check":"dependency","message":"uv (Astral Python runner) not found - required - install: https://docs.astral.sh/uv/","severity":"error","tool":"uv"}
```

and warnings for `task`, `go`, and `node`.

## Expected
In Linux `--yes` / non-interactive mode, the installer should either:

1. bootstrap required core tools where safe/portable, at least `uv`, `task`, and `gh`; or
2. fail loud before claiming install success if required post-install doctor dependencies cannot be satisfied automatically; or
3. clearly document that Linux tool bootstrap is not implemented and that `--yes` only reports missing tools.

The current message says consent is implied by `--yes`, which suggests the installer should take action rather than only emit fallback instructions.

## Notes
This came from setting up a fresh WSL maintainer lane to verify the Linux installer path. The installer payload deposit worked; the issue is the expected core-tool bootstrap behavior after detection.
narratives.ImplementationPlan:
1. Update the installer source file cmd/deft-install/setup.go around EnsureCoreTools so Linux non-interactive installs handle missing uv, task, and gh with a deterministic bootstrap or a fail-loud unsupported-bootstrap result instead of success-plus-fallback prose.
2. Add focused test fixtures and assertions in cmd/deft-install/setup_test.go that exercise the Linux missing-tool path with injectable command/probe seams, verifying JSON missing_tools, user-facing output, and doctor-handoff evidence without invoking real package managers.
narratives.UserStory: As a Linux adopter, I want deft-install --yes to leave required Deft tools installed or fail loudly, so that my first doctor run is actionable and not silently broken.

items.title: Bootstrap or fail-loud for missing Linux core tools in non-interactive installer runs

narrative.Acceptance: Given a fresh Linux or WSL environment missing uv, task, and gh, when install-linux-amd64 --yes --repo-root <project> --json runs and the supported bootstrap path is available, then the installer installs or wires those required tools and the JSON result does not leave them in missing_tools.

items.title: Report unsupported Linux bootstrap as a deterministic installer failure

narrative.Acceptance: Given a Linux environment where the installer cannot safely bootstrap required tools, when the non-interactive install reaches the core-tool phase, then it exits non-zero with structured actionable output instead of printing consent implied by --yes and still claiming install success.

items.title: Add focused installer tests for the Linux missing-tool branch

narrative.Acceptance: Given mocked tool discovery and bootstrap outcomes, the installer test suite asserts the success, failure, and fallback-output behavior for Linux --yes missing-tool handling without requiring real apt, brew, network access, or root privileges.

references.title: Issue #1538: Linux installer --yes reports missing core tools instead of bootstrapping them

expected_outputs: focused Go installer tests cover Linux missing-tool bootstrap or fail-loud behavior
full task check remains green after the installer change
